evidence_quotes.quote: Welcome to the first week of our Public Speaking class! This week is split between our course orientation and learning about some core foundations regarding the concept of public speaking, its evolution, and how that dynamic looks different in a virtual space as opposed to your standard face-to-face environment. Our specific objectives for this week include the following: Identify and describe key models of communication (transactional & interactional) Distinguish between public speaking best practices in a virtual versus on site environment Identify and describe rhetorical proofs, as well as rhetorical canons Compare diverse global perspectives on Public Speaking.

Welcome to the second week of the course! Here's what we're aiming to accomplish this week: Develop & deliver an extemporaneous speech in a virtual environment. Identify and describe the types and root causes of communication apprehension. Create an anxiety management plan to address communication apprehension during the course and beyond. Distinguish between the different modes of speech delivery (extemporaneous, impromptu, manuscript, etc.)

Welcome to week 3! As we prepare for the next speech, we need to take our audience into account more carefully. Ask questions such as "what topics will they find interesting" and "how can I make this matter to them?" With that, we're focusing on the following objectives for this week: Identify the different methods of audience analysis for presentations. Evaluate and provide feedback on topic proposals from peers Develop an assessment tool to analyze a target audience for an upcoming speech.

Welcome to week 4! As we get closer to the next presentation, the preparation steps for your presentation become more important: This presentation is longer in duration and the outline I'm having you submit is a more thorough, formal one. As such, you should be considering questions like "what is the most effective way to arrange my speech" or "how might my audience (your peers) connect with this message". Bearing that in mind, here our the objectives for this week: Identify and describe elements of effective speech introductions, transitions and conclusions. Create a draft of a presentation outline utilizing one of the common speech organizational pattern Deploy an assessment tool to analyze a target audience for an upcoming speech.

rubric_findings.suggested_fix: While objectives are consistently present and measurable, consider adding explicit statements at the beginning of each module that directly state 'By the end of this module, you will be able to...' to make the learning outcomes even more prominent and student-centered.
rubric_findings.reasoning: Module learning objectives are consistently placed at the beginning of each module (in the 'Week X Overview' pages under the 'Objectives' tab). The objectives are written using measurable action verbs such as 'Identify,' 'Describe,' 'Develop,' 'Distinguish,' 'Create,' 'Evaluate,' and 'Compare,' which align with Bloom's taxonomy. The objectives are specific and measurable (e.g., 'Identify and describe key models of communication,' 'Develop & deliver an extemporaneous speech'). While they are not always framed in complete student-centered language (e.g., 'Students will be able to...'), they clearly describe what students will do and accomplish. The objectives align with the course's overall focus on public speaking skills and progress logically from basic concepts (Week 1) to more complex tasks (developing presentations with audience analysis and organizational patterns). The consistent placement and structure across all reviewed modules meets the 'aligned' criteria.
